content:
$$ \text { Match the following types of waves with their wavelength ranges } $$

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Waves } & \text { Wavelength ranges } \\ \hline \text { i. Microwave } & \text { a. } 700 \mathrm{~nm} \text { to } 400 \mathrm{~nm} \\ \hline \text { ii. Visible light } & \text { b. } 1 \mathrm{~nm} \text { to } 10^{-3} \mathrm{~nm} \\ \hline \text { iii. Ultraviolet } & \text { c. } 0.1 \mathrm{~m} \text { to } 1 \mathrm{~mm} \\ \hline \text { iv. X-rays } & \text { d. } 400 \mathrm{~nm} \text { to } 1 \mathrm{~nm} \\ \hline \end{array} $$
explanation:
The correct matching is

i. Microwave → c. 0.1 m to 1 mm

ii. Visible light → a. 700 nm to 400 nm

iii. Ultraviolet → d. 400 nm to 1 nm

iv. X‐rays → b. 1 nm to 10⁻³ nm

That corresponds to Option A.

content: The total energy carried by the light wave when it travels from a rarer to a non-reflecting and nonabsorbing medium

options.content: either increases or decreases depending upon angle of incidence

explanation:
When light passes into a non-reflecting, non-absorbing medium, no energy is lost at the boundary. In particular:

Energy flux is given by the (time-averaged) Poynting vector

$$\vec S = \vec E \times \vec H\,. $$

For no reflection and no absorption, the tangential components of both fields are continuous across the interface:

$$E_1=E_2,\quad H_1=H_2\,. $$

Hence the incident and transmitted fluxes are equal:

$$S_1 = E_1H_1 = E_2H_2 = S_2\,. $$

Since all the energy that arrives is carried forward, the total energy carried by the wave remains the same.

Answer: Option A (remains same).

content: Electromagnetic waves are incident normally on a perfectly reflecting surface having surface area $A$. If $I$ is the intensity of the incident electromagnetic radiation and $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum, the force exerted by the electromagnetic wave on the reflecting surface is
explanation:
The force exerted by electromagnetic waves on a perfectly reflecting surface can be derived using the concept of radiation pressure. When electromagnetic waves are incident on a reflecting surface, they exert a pressure due to the change in momentum as they reflect.

For a perfectly reflecting surface, the radiation pressure $P$ is given by:

$$ P = \frac{2I}{c} $$

where:

$I$ is the intensity of the incident radiation,

$c$ is the speed of light.

The force $F$ exerted on a surface due to radiation pressure is given by multiplying the radiation pressure by the surface area $A$:

$$ F = P \times A = \frac{2I}{c} \times A = \frac{2IA}{c} $$

Therefore, the correct expression for the force exerted by the electromagnetic wave on the reflecting surface is:

Option A

$$ \frac{2 I A}{c} $$

content: The ratio of the magnitudes of electric field to the magnetic field of an electromagnetic wave is of the order of
explanation:
As we known,

$$v=\frac{\mathbf{E}}{\mathbf{B}} \text { or } v=\frac{|\mathbf{E}|}{|\mathbf{B}|}$$

For an electromagnetic wave, $$v=c$$

$$\therefore \frac{|\mathbf{E}|}{|\mathbf{B}|}=c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$

$$\therefore|\mathbf{E}|:|\mathbf{B}|$$ is of the order of $$10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$.

content: Which of the following radiations of electromagnetic waves has the highest wavelength ?
explanation:
Increasing order of wavelength of given electromagnetic wave is given as

$$\lambda_{\text {X-rays }}<\lambda_{\text {UV-rays }}<\lambda_{\text {IR-rays }}<\lambda_{\text {microwaves }}$$

Hence, microwaves has highest wavelengths.

content: Suppose that the electric field amplitude of electromagnetic wave is $$E_0=120 \mathrm{~NC}^{-1}$$ and its frequency $$f=50 \mathrm{~MHz}$$. Then, which of the following value is incorrectly computed?

options.content: Angular frequency of EM wave is $$\pi \times 10^8 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$$.

explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} E_0 & =120 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \\ f & =50 \mathrm{MHz}=50 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Hz} \end{aligned}$$

(a) magnetic field amplitude,

$$\begin{aligned} B_0 & =\frac{E_0}{c}=\frac{120}{3 \times 10^8}=40 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~T} \\ & =400 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{T} \end{aligned}$$

(b) Angular frequency, $$\omega=2 \pi f=2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^6$$

$$=\pi \times 10^8 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}$$

(c) Propagation constant,

$$k=\frac{\omega}{c}=\frac{\pi \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8}=1.047 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{m}$$

(d) Wavelength, $$\lambda=\frac{c}{f}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{50 \times 10^6}=\frac{30}{5}=6 \mathrm{~m}$$

content: The source of electromagnetic wave can be a charge

options.content: moving parallel to the magnetic field

explanation: Electromagnetic waves are produced by accelerated charges. In given options, only a charge particle moving in a circular orbit is in accelerated motion. So, it produces electromagnetic wave.

content: A light beam of intensity $$20 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{cm}^2$$ is incident normally on a perfectly reflecting surface of sides $$25 \mathrm{~cm} \times 15 \mathrm{~cm}$$. The momentum imparted to the surface by the light per second is
explanation:
Given, intensity, $$I=20 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{cm}^2$$

Area of surface, $$A=(25 \times 15) \mathrm{cm}^2$$

and time, $$t=1 \mathrm{~s}$$

The intensity of light of energy $$E$$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\frac{E}{A t} \Rightarrow 20=\frac{E}{25 \times 15 \times 1} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & =20 \times 25 \times 15 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

The momentum imparted to a perfectly reflecting surface is given by

$$p=\frac{2 E}{c}=\frac{2 \times 20 \times 25 \times 15}{3 \times 10^8}=5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$

content: An antenna uses electromagnetic waves of frequency $$5 \mathrm{~MHz}$$. For proper working, the size of the antenna should be
explanation:
Given, Frequency of wave, $$f=5 \mathrm{~MHz}=5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Hz}$$

$$\therefore$$ Wavelength, $$\lambda=\frac{c}{f}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^6}=60 \mathrm{~m}$$

For proper working, the size of antenna $$l$$ should be $$\frac{\lambda}{4}$$

i.e. $$\quad l=\frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{60}{4}=15 \mathrm{~m}$$

content: Due to Doppler's effect the shift in wavelength observed is $$0.1 \mathop A\limits^o$$ for a star producing wavelength $$6000 \mathop A\limits^o$$. Velocity of recession of the star will be
explanation:
Given, Doppler's shift, $$\Delta \lambda=0 \mathrm{l}\mathop A\limits^o$$

$$=0.1 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}=10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$$

wavelength, $$\lambda=6000 \mathop A\limits^o=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$$

If $$v_s$$ is the velocity of recession of the star and $$c$$ is velocity of light, then Doppler's shift

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \lambda =\left(\frac{v_s}{c}\right) \lambda \Rightarrow v_s=\frac{c \cdot \Delta \lambda}{\lambda}=\frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-11}}{6 \times 10^{-7}} \\ =0.5 \times 10^4=5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

content: An electromagnetic wave is travelling in $$x$$-direction with electric field vector given by, $$\mathbf{E}_y=E_0 \sin (k x-\omega t) \hat{\mathbf{j}}$$. The correct expression for magnetic field vector is
explanation:
As in Electromagnetic wave, v, E and B are mutually perpendicular,

$$\therefore \mathbf{B}$$ is along $$Z$$ - axis

So, $$\quad \mathbf{B}_z=B_0 \sin (k x-\omega t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

further, $$B_0=\frac{E_0}{C}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathbf{B}_z=\frac{E_0}{C} \sin (k x-\omega t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

content: The phenomenon involved in the reflection of radio-waves by ionosphere is similar to

options.content: reflection of light by plane mirror
total internal reflection of light in air during a mirage
dispersion of light by water molecules during the formation
scattering of light by air particles

explanation: Radio waves (frequencies upto $$30 \mathrm{~MHz}$$ ) propagate from transmitters as sky waves le they are totally reflected by the ionosphere layer of atmosphere, (after which they are receieved by various receivers) so phenomena is similar to TIR (Total Internal Reflection) of light in air resulting in mirage effect.

content: If $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ represent electric and magnetic field vectors of an electromagentic wave, the direction of propagation of the wave is along
explanation: According to electromagnetic wave property, the electric field is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The direction of electromagnetic wave propagation will perpendicular to $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{B}$. then $\mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{B}=\mathbf{C}$
